walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Point Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Let's get Century: Golem Edition set up. First, find the deck of point cards. These are the cards with beautiful golem illustrations and victory point values on them. Give the deck a thorough shuffle and place it face down in the center of the table where everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Point Cards
walkthrough.text: Now flip five point cards face up from the top of the deck and lay them in a row from left to right. This is the point card row. During the game, players will claim golems from this row by spending crystals. As cards are claimed, the row slides left and a new card fills the rightmost space from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Place the Bonus Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the copper and silver victory point tokens. Place one stack of copper tokens directly above the leftmost point card in the row, and one stack of silver tokens above the second point card. Whenever a player claims the leftmost point card, they also take a copper token worth 3 bonus points. When they claim the second card, they get a silver token worth 1 point. These tokens are a nice incentive to grab those first two golems.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player gets two starting cards that are the same for everyone. Find the cards labeled Create 2 and Upgrade 2. Create 2 lets you gain two yellow crystals from the supply. Upgrade 2 lets you upgrade any two of your crystals, each by one level. Every player takes one copy of each of these cards into their hand. These are your engine's foundation.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Merchant Deck
walkthrough.text: Now find the merchant card deck. These are the cards with crystal icons and arrows showing trades or gains. Shuffle this deck thoroughly and set it face down near the point card row. This is the merchant deck, and it is how players will build their crystal-converting engine throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Merchant Row
walkthrough.text: Flip six merchant cards face up from the top of the merchant deck and lay them in a row from left to right. This is the merchant row. During the game, players will acquire cards from this row to add to their hand. The leftmost card is free to take, but cards further to the right cost crystals to acquire. We will explain that cost when we cover the rules.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Crystal Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the crystal tokens in the center of the table, sorted by color. There are four types in ascending value: yellow, green, turquoise, and magenta. Yellow is the most basic and magenta is the most valuable. You will be collecting, upgrading, and trading these throughout the game. Make sure everyone can reach the supply easily.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Caravan Cards and Starting Crystals
walkthrough.text: Give each player a caravan card. This is the long card where you place your crystals to track what you own. Now hand out starting crystals based on turn order. The first player gets three yellow crystals. The second player gets four yellow crystals. The third player gets four yellow crystals. If there is a fourth player, they get three yellow and one green. If there is a fifth player, they also get three yellow and one green. Place your starting crystals on your caravan card. Remember, your caravan can hold a maximum of 10 crystals at any time.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first however you like. Play proceeds clockwise. The player order matters slightly because of the different starting crystal counts, which balance out the advantage of going first. Once everyone has their caravan card, starting crystals, and two starting cards in hand, you are ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Century: Golem Edition is beautifully simple. On your turn, you do exactly one of four actions: Play a card from your hand, Acquire a merchant card from the row, Rest to pick up all your played cards, or Claim a point card by spending crystals. That is it. One action, then the next player goes. Let's walk through each action.

walkthrough.title: Play a Card — Crystal Cards
walkthrough.text: The first action is Play a Card. You take one card from your hand and place it face up in front of you in your played area. Then you do what the card says. There are three types of merchant cards. The first type is crystal cards. These show crystal icons with no arrows. When you play a crystal card, you simply take the shown crystals from the supply and add them to your caravan. For example, your starting Create 2 card gives you two yellow crystals.

walkthrough.title: Play a Card — Upgrade Cards
walkthrough.text: The second type is upgrade cards. These show an upward arrow with a number. When you play an upgrade card, you upgrade that many of your crystals, each by exactly one level. The upgrade path is yellow to green, green to turquoise, turquoise to magenta. You return the old crystal to the supply and take the new one. For example, your starting Upgrade 2 card lets you upgrade any two crystals on your caravan by one step each. You could upgrade two yellows to two greens, or one yellow to green and one green to turquoise.

walkthrough.title: Play a Card — Trade Cards
walkthrough.text: The third type is trade cards. These show crystals on the left, an arrow, and different crystals on the right. When you play a trade card, you return the crystals shown on the left to the supply and take the crystals shown on the right. Here is the key: trade cards are repeatable. You can execute the trade as many times as you want in a single play, as long as you have the crystals to pay each time. So if a card says trade two yellows for one green, and you have six yellows, you could do that trade three times in one play to get three greens.

walkthrough.title: Acquire a Merchant Card
walkthrough.text: The second action is Acquire. Instead of playing a card, you take one merchant card from the face-up row and add it to your hand. The leftmost card in the row is always free. But if you want a card further to the right, you must pay a cost: place one crystal from your caravan onto each card that sits to the left of the one you want. Any crystal color works for payment. After taking your card, slide all remaining cards to the left and fill the empty rightmost space from the merchant deck. Important: if a card in the row has crystals on it from other players' payments, you take those crystals too when you acquire that card. Free bonus crystals.

walkthrough.title: Rest
walkthrough.text: The third action is Rest. When you play cards, they go to your played area and you cannot use them again until you rest. When you choose the Rest action, you simply pick up all of your played cards and return them to your hand. That is your entire turn. You cannot rest if you have no played cards. Timing your rest turns is important because resting means you are not doing anything productive that turn, but you need your cards back to keep your engine running.

walkthrough.title: Claim a Point Card
walkthrough.text: The fourth and most important action is Claim. This is how you score points and win the game. To claim a point card from the row, you must have the exact crystals shown on that card sitting on your caravan. Return those crystals to the supply and take the point card, placing it face down in your score pile. If you claimed the leftmost point card, also take a copper token worth 3 bonus points. If you claimed the second point card, take a silver token worth 1 point. After claiming, slide the remaining point cards to the left and reveal a new one from the deck to fill the rightmost space.

walkthrough.title: Caravan Limit
walkthrough.text: One rule to always keep in mind: your caravan can never hold more than 10 crystals. If gaining or trading crystals would put you over 10, you must return excess crystals of your choice to the supply. Plan your moves carefully so you do not waste crystals by bumping into this limit. This cap also means hoarding crystals is not a great strategy since you have limited carrying capacity.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game end is triggered when any player claims their fifth point card in a four- or five-player game, or their sixth point card in a two- or three-player game. When this happens, finish the current round so that every player has taken the same number of turns. The player who triggered the end still finishes their turn normally.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Once the round is complete, everyone counts up their score. Add together the victory point values printed on all your claimed point cards, plus 3 points for each copper token, plus 1 point for each silver token, plus 1 point for each non-yellow crystal remaining on your caravan. Yellow crystals are worth nothing. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player among the tied players who took their turn last in the final round wins.

summary.title: Turn Overview
bullets: On your turn, perform exactly 1 of 4 actions
Play a Card, Acquire, Rest, or Claim

summary.title: Play a Card — Trade Cards

Trades are REPEATABLE — execute multiple times per play if you can pay
Example: trade 2 yellow for 1 green, repeat 3x with 6 yellows

If you would exceed 10, return excess crystals of your choice

2-3 players: triggered when someone claims their 6th point card
Finish the current round so all players have equal turns

bullets: Point card values + copper tokens (3 pts each) + silver tokens (1 pt each)
Plus 1 point per non-yellow crystal remaining on caravan
Highest total wins; tiebreak: last player to take a turn

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a practice round with three players: Alex, Blake, and Casey. Alex goes first with 3 yellow crystals, Blake is second with 4 yellow crystals, and Casey is third also with 4 yellow crystals. Each player has the same two starting cards in hand: Create 2 and Upgrade 2. The merchant row has six face-up cards and the point card row has five face-up golems.

walkthrough.title: Setting the Scene
walkthrough.text: Let's say the merchant row, from left to right, has these six cards: Card 1 is a crystal card that gains 1 green. Card 2 is a trade card that converts 2 yellow into 1 turquoise. Card 3 is an upgrade card with Upgrade 3. Card 4 is a trade card that converts 1 green and 1 yellow into 1 magenta. Card 5 is a crystal card that gains 2 green. Card 6 is a trade card that converts 3 yellow into 1 magenta. The first two point cards in the row require relatively cheap crystal combinations.

walkthrough.title: Alex's Turn — Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Alex decides to start building crystals. Alex plays Create 2 from hand, placing it face up in the played area. Alex takes 2 yellow crystals from the supply and adds them to the caravan. Alex now has 5 yellow crystals total and one card in the played area.

walkthrough.title: Blake's Turn — Acquire a Merchant Card
walkthrough.text: Blake eyes that trade card in position 2 that converts 2 yellow into 1 turquoise. That is a powerful conversion. Position 2 means Blake needs to pay 1 crystal on the card to its left. Blake places 1 yellow crystal from the caravan onto Card 1 in the merchant row. Then Blake takes Card 2 into hand. Blake now has 3 yellow crystals on the caravan and three cards in hand: Create 2, Upgrade 2, and the new trade card.

walkthrough.title: Blake's Mistake — Forgetting to Pay
walkthrough.text: Wait, let's rewind. What if Blake had tried to just grab Card 2 without placing a crystal on Card 1? That would be illegal. You must place one crystal from your caravan on each card to the left of the one you want. The leftmost card is the only free one. Blake could have taken Card 1 for free, but wanted Card 2, so a payment of one crystal on Card 1 is required. Always count the cards to the left and pay one crystal each.

walkthrough.title: Casey's Turn — Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Casey decides to play Create 2 as well. Casey places it in the played area and takes 2 yellow crystals from the supply. Casey now has 6 yellow crystals on the caravan. A solid stockpile to work with.

walkthrough.title: Alex's Turn — Upgrade Crystals
walkthrough.text: Alex plays Upgrade 2 from hand. Alex chooses to upgrade 2 yellow crystals to 2 green crystals. Alex returns the 2 yellows to the supply and takes 2 greens. Alex's caravan now has: 3 yellow and 2 green crystals. Both starting cards are now in the played area, so Alex's hand is empty.

walkthrough.title: Blake's Turn — Play the Trade Card
walkthrough.text: Blake plays the newly acquired trade card: convert 2 yellow into 1 turquoise. Blake returns 2 yellow to the supply and takes 1 turquoise. Blake's caravan now has 1 yellow and 1 turquoise.

walkthrough.title: Blake's Mistake — Forgetting Trades Are Repeatable
walkthrough.text: Hold on. Blake had 3 yellow crystals before playing the trade card but only did the trade once. Remember, trade cards are repeatable. Blake could have done the trade once for 2 yellow, leaving 1 yellow and 1 turquoise. But Blake could not do it a second time because that would require 2 more yellow, and Blake only has 1 yellow left. So in this case, one trade was the maximum. But if Blake had 4 yellow, the trade could have been executed twice to get 2 turquoise. Always check if you can repeat for extra value.

walkthrough.title: Casey's Turn — Acquire a Card
walkthrough.text: Casey sees a nice crystal card in position 1 of the merchant row. Remember, the old Card 1 was taken by Blake, so everything slid left and a new card filled the right end. The current leftmost card is the old Card 1 position, which now has Blake's yellow crystal sitting on it from the earlier payment. Casey takes this leftmost card for free and also collects the yellow crystal that was sitting on it. Casey now has 7 yellow crystals and a new card in hand. Free crystals from the merchant row are a nice perk.

walkthrough.title: Alex's Turn — Rest
walkthrough.text: Alex has no cards in hand. Both starting cards are in the played area. Alex's only option for a productive turn right now is to Rest. Alex picks up both Create 2 and Upgrade 2 from the played area back into hand. That is Alex's whole turn. No crystals gained, no cards acquired, but now Alex can play cards again next turn.

walkthrough.title: Why Rest Matters
walkthrough.text: Notice how Alex had to spend a whole turn just picking up cards. This is why acquiring more merchant cards is important. The more cards you have in hand, the more turns you can take before needing to rest. A player with five or six cards in hand can go many turns before resting, while a player with only two cards will rest frequently. Building a bigger hand is like building a better engine.

walkthrough.title: Blake's Turn — Rest
walkthrough.text: Blake is in the same situation. Both starting cards and the trade card are in the played area. Blake chooses to Rest and picks up all three cards. Blake now has three cards in hand: Create 2, Upgrade 2, and the 2-yellow-for-1-turquoise trade card. More cards means more flexibility before the next rest.

walkthrough.title: Casey's Turn — Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Casey plays Upgrade 2. Casey upgrades 2 yellow crystals to 2 green. Casey returns the yellows to the supply and takes greens. Casey's caravan now shows: 5 yellow and 2 green crystals. Casey still has the newly acquired crystal card in hand plus one starting card in the played area.

walkthrough.title: Alex's Turn — Play Create 2 Again
walkthrough.text: Alex plays Create 2 again and takes 2 more yellow crystals. Alex's caravan now has: 5 yellow and 2 green crystals. Alex is building up a nice supply. Next turn Alex could upgrade more, or maybe start looking at which point cards are within reach.

walkthrough.title: Checking the Point Card Row
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what is available. Say the leftmost point card costs 2 green and 1 yellow for 6 victory points. The second point card costs 1 turquoise and 2 yellow for 8 victory points. Alex has 5 yellow and 2 green, which matches the leftmost card exactly. But should Alex claim it now or keep building?

walkthrough.title: Blake's Turn — Building the Engine
walkthrough.text: Blake plays Create 2 to gain 2 yellow crystals. Blake's caravan is now 3 yellow and 1 turquoise. Blake is starting to accumulate good resources. With the trade card, Blake can convert yellows to turquoise efficiently on a future turn.

walkthrough.title: Casey's Turn — Play the New Card
walkthrough.text: Casey plays the crystal card acquired earlier. Let's say it gains 1 green crystal. Casey takes 1 green from the supply. Casey's caravan is now 5 yellow and 3 green crystals. Casey is getting close to 10 crystals and should think about claiming a point card soon before hitting the caravan limit.

walkthrough.title: Alex Claims a Point Card
walkthrough.text: Alex decides to go for it. Alex chooses the Claim action and targets the leftmost point card, which costs 2 green and 1 yellow. Alex returns 2 green and 1 yellow to the supply and takes the point card worth 6 victory points. Because it was the leftmost card, Alex also takes a copper token worth 3 bonus points. That is 9 points from a single claim. Nice. The remaining point cards slide left and a new one is revealed from the deck.

walkthrough.title: The Round Continues
walkthrough.text: The game keeps going around the table. Blake and Casey each take their turns, and then a new round begins. Players continue playing cards, acquiring new merchant cards, resting when they run out of hand cards, and claiming point cards when they can afford them. The race is on to claim point cards efficiently while building a powerful hand of merchant cards.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen all four actions in play: Play a Card to gain, upgrade, or trade crystals. Acquire to add merchant cards to your hand and sometimes collect bonus crystals. Rest to pick up your played cards. And Claim to spend crystals and score point cards with golem illustrations. The whole game flows from these four simple actions. The strategy comes from deciding which action to take each turn and how to build your card engine.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to crystal types, card types, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for quick-reference on terms and card types
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers

walkthrough.title: Do Not Hoard Crystals
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to stockpile crystals on your caravan, but resist the urge. Your caravan maxes out at 10, and yellow crystals are worth zero at the end. Every turn you spend accumulating crystals without a plan is a turn your opponents might use to claim a point card. Have a target golem in mind and work toward it efficiently.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Efficiently
walkthrough.text: The upgrade path goes yellow, green, turquoise, magenta. Each upgrade step moves one level. A card that says Upgrade 3 does not jump a crystal three levels. It upgrades three crystals, each by one level. So Upgrade 3 could turn three yellows into three greens, or two yellows into two greens and one green into one turquoise. Think about which combination gets you closest to the point card you want.

walkthrough.title: Acquire Powerful Trade Cards Early
walkthrough.text: In the early game, prioritize acquiring strong trade and upgrade cards over claiming cheap point cards. A good trade card that you play five or six times across the game generates far more value than rushing a low-scoring golem. Cards that convert efficiently, like trading two yellows for a turquoise, or one green for one magenta, are engines that keep paying off.

walkthrough.title: Time Your Rests
walkthrough.text: Resting costs a full turn, so you want to minimize how often you do it. One way is to acquire more merchant cards so your hand lasts longer before running out. Another trick is to rest strategically when the point card row does not have anything you can claim yet anyway. Do not rest when a perfect point card is sitting there waiting for you.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Need
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to your opponents' caravans. If someone has three turquoise crystals and the leftmost point card costs three turquoise, they are probably going to claim it next turn. You might want to rush and claim a different card, or grab a merchant card they want before they can. The game has more interaction than it first appears.

walkthrough.title: Chase Copper Tokens Early
walkthrough.text: Copper tokens are worth 3 bonus points each. That is a huge swing. If you can claim the leftmost point card at a reasonable crystal cost, do it. Early copper tokens can be the difference between winning and losing. Silver tokens at 1 point are nice but not worth going out of your way for. Focus on copper when the leftmost golem's cost is affordable.

walkthrough.title: Balance Your Engine and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The biggest beginner mistake is spending too long building the perfect engine and never scoring. Your opponents will claim five or six point cards while you are still tinkering with trade combos. Aim to acquire two or three strong merchant cards in the early game, then shift to claiming point cards. You do not need a perfect engine. You need a good-enough engine that scores fast.

walkthrough.title: Keep Non-Yellow Crystals at Game End
walkthrough.text: Remember that every non-yellow crystal on your caravan at game end is worth 1 point. If the game is about to end and you have a turn left, consider upgrading your yellows to greens for a few extra points. It is a small bonus, but close games are often decided by one or two points. Do not underestimate the value of leftover turquoise or magenta crystals.

summary.title: Do Not Hoard Crystals
bullets: Caravan limit is 10; yellow crystals are worth 0 at game end
Always have a target point card in mind when collecting

summary.title: Upgrade Efficiently
bullets: Each upgrade moves ONE level (yellow -> green -> turquoise -> magenta)
Upgrade 3 = upgrade three crystals by one step each, not one crystal by three steps

summary.title: Acquire Powerful Trade Cards Early
bullets: Strong trade/upgrade cards generate value over many plays
Prioritize engine building early over rushing cheap point cards

summary.title: Time Your Rests
bullets: Rest costs a full turn — minimize frequency by building a bigger hand
Rest when the point card row has nothing you can claim

summary.title: Watch What Opponents Need
bullets: Check opponents' caravans for clues about their next claim
Grab merchant cards or point cards before opponents can

summary.title: Chase Copper Tokens Early
bullets: Copper tokens = 3 bonus VP — a significant swing
Claim leftmost point cards when the crystal cost is reasonable

summary.title: Balance Your Engine and Scoring
bullets: Biggest mistake: over-building your engine, never scoring
Acquire 2-3 strong merchant cards, then shift to claiming golems

summary.title: Keep Non-Yellow Crystals at Game End
bullets: Each non-yellow crystal = 1 VP at game end
Upgrade leftover yellows to greens on your final turns for bonus points

walkthrough.title: Build Trade Chains
walkthrough.text: The strongest engines in Century: Golem Edition are trade chains. A trade chain is when you have multiple cards that convert crystals in sequence. For example, one card produces four yellows, another converts two yellows to a turquoise, and a third converts a turquoise and a yellow to a magenta. Playing these in sequence over three turns, with a rest in between cycles, lets you reliably produce high-value crystals to claim expensive golems.

walkthrough.title: Value Conversion Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Not all trade cards are created equal. The best cards have a high output-to-input ratio in terms of upgrade levels. Consider: trading 2 yellow for 1 turquoise is a net gain of 2 upgrade levels for 2 crystals. But trading 3 yellow for 1 turquoise is a net gain of 2 for 3 crystals. Always evaluate how many upgrade levels a trade card gives you relative to what it costs. The most efficient converters win games.

walkthrough.title: Optimal Upgrade Paths
walkthrough.text: Upgrade cards are most efficient when you upgrade the cheapest crystals. Upgrading a yellow to green costs one upgrade point and gains one level. This is the same rate whether you upgrade yellow to green or turquoise to magenta. But consider what you need: if a point card costs two magenta, you need to climb the full ladder. Sometimes it is more efficient to trade directly for magenta using a trade card rather than upgrading step by step. Compare the action cost of each path.

walkthrough.title: When to Rush the Game End
walkthrough.text: Rushing the game end is a valid strategy when you have more point cards than your opponents and the available golems are cheap. If you have four point cards and everyone else has two, claiming a fifth ends the game immediately in your favor even if your individual cards are lower scoring. Watch the point card count carefully and calculate whether ending the game now gives you the win versus letting opponents catch up.

walkthrough.title: When to Score Big Instead
walkthrough.text: The opposite of rushing is the big-score strategy. If expensive golems worth 15 to 20 points are in the row, and you have a powerful engine that can produce magenta crystals reliably, it might be worth letting opponents grab cheap 6-point cards while you set up for a massive claim. Two 17-point golems with copper tokens can outscore four 8-point golems. Know which strategy the game state favors.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Merchant Row
walkthrough.text: Before acquiring a card, think about what it does for your engine. Does it fill a gap? If you produce lots of yellow but have no way to convert them, a trade card is crucial. If you can already convert well but run out of crystals, a crystal production card helps. Also consider the cost of acquisition. A great card in position 5 costs four crystals to acquire. That might not be worth it if a decent card in position 1 is free.

walkthrough.title: Timing Copper and Silver Tokens
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track the copper and silver tokens carefully. When the leftmost point card is easy to claim, everyone will race for the copper token. If you are not in position to claim it this turn, consider whether you can claim the second card for a silver token instead. Sometimes it is better to let an opponent take the copper and grab a higher-VP card in position 3 or 4 that nobody else is targeting.

walkthrough.title: Hand Size Optimization
walkthrough.text: The ideal hand size is around four to six cards. Fewer than four means you rest too often. More than six means you probably spent too many turns acquiring cards instead of scoring. Every Acquire action is a turn you did not Play, Rest, or Claim. Find the balance point where your hand lets you play three to five turns between rests and your cards efficiently produce the crystals you need.

walkthrough.title: Deny Opponents Key Cards
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best Acquire is not the card that helps you the most, but the one that hurts your opponent. If someone has a trade chain that is one card away from being dominant, consider grabbing that card even if it is only decent for you. This is especially true if the card is cheap or free. Denying a critical piece of an opponent's engine can be worth more than a marginal improvement to yours.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Crystal Management
walkthrough.text: In the last few turns, every crystal matters. Convert all your yellows to greens or higher if possible, since non-yellow crystals are each worth a point. If you cannot claim another golem, use your remaining turns to upgrade crystals for endgame scoring. Also consider whether you can claim one more cheap golem. Even a 5-point golem with a copper token is 8 points, which almost certainly beats upgrading a few crystals.

summary.title: Build Trade Chains
bullets: Acquire cards that convert crystals in sequence across multiple plays
Example: produce yellow -> trade to turquoise -> trade to magenta

summary.title: Value Conversion Efficiency
bullets: Evaluate trade cards by net upgrade levels gained vs. crystals spent
2 yellow for 1 turquoise (net +2 levels for 2 crystals) > 3 yellow for 1 turquoise

summary.title: Optimal Upgrade Paths
bullets: Compare upgrade cards vs. trade cards for reaching the same crystal
Direct trades to magenta can be more action-efficient than step-by-step upgrades

summary.title: When to Rush the Game End
bullets: Rush if you lead in point card count and cheap golems are available
Claiming your 6th card (3-player) ends the game even with lower individual scores

summary.title: When to Score Big Instead
bullets: Target expensive 15-20 VP golems if your engine produces magenta reliably
Two big scores + copper tokens can outscore many small claims

summary.title: Reading the Merchant Row
bullets: Acquire cards that fill gaps in your engine (production vs. conversion)
Factor in acquisition cost — a free decent card may beat an expensive great card

summary.title: Timing Copper and Silver Tokens
bullets: Track who can claim leftmost/second cards this turn
Sometimes skip the race for copper and grab a higher-VP card further right

summary.title: Hand Size Optimization
bullets: Ideal hand: 4-6 cards; fewer = too many rests, more = too many acquires
Aim for 3-5 productive turns between each rest

summary.title: Deny Opponents Key Cards
bullets: Grab cards that complete an opponent's engine, especially if cheap/free
Denying a critical piece can outweigh a marginal gain to your own engine

summary.title: Late-Game Crystal Management
bullets: Upgrade all yellows to greens+ for endgame crystal scoring (1 pt each)
A cheap golem + copper token (8+ pts) almost always beats upgrading leftover crystals

entries.definition: The most basic crystal. Worth 0 points at game end. Used as the starting resource and foundation for upgrading and trading.
The second-tier crystal. Upgraded from yellow. Worth 1 point at game end if remaining on your caravan.
The third-tier crystal. Upgraded from green. Worth 1 point at game end. Required for many mid-to-high value point cards.
The highest-tier crystal. Upgraded from turquoise. Worth 1 point at game end. Required for the most valuable point cards.
The fixed sequence for crystal upgrades: yellow to green to turquoise to magenta. Each upgrade moves exactly one step. Crystals cannot be downgraded.
Your personal crystal storage card. Holds a maximum of 10 crystals at any time. All crystals on your caravan are public information visible to all players.
The shared pool of crystals in the center of the table. Crystals are taken from and returned to this supply throughout the game.
One of the four actions. Place a card from your hand into your played area and execute its effect (gain crystals, upgrade crystals, or trade crystals).
One of the four actions. Take a merchant card from the face-up row into your hand. The leftmost card is free; each position further right costs one crystal placed on each card to its left.
One of the four actions. Pick up all cards from your played area and return them to your hand. Cannot be done if you have no played cards. Uses your entire turn.

One of the four actions. Return crystals matching a point card's cost from your caravan to the supply, then take that point card into your score pile. Triggers card sliding and bonus tokens if applicable.
A type of merchant card that shows crystal icons with no arrows. When played, gain the depicted crystals from the supply. Example: Create 2 gains 2 yellow crystals.
A type of merchant card with an upward arrow and a number. When played, upgrade that many crystals on your caravan by one level each. Upgrades can be split across different crystals.

A type of merchant card showing crystals on the left, an arrow, and crystals on the right. Return the left-side crystals and gain the right-side crystals. Trades are repeatable: execute multiple times per play if you can pay.
A card with a golem illustration and a victory point value. Shows a crystal cost that must be paid to claim it. Claimed cards go to your score pile and count toward the game end trigger.
The two cards every player begins with: Create 2 (gain 2 yellow crystals) and Upgrade 2 (upgrade 2 crystals by 1 level each). These form the foundation of every player's engine.

The face-up row of 6 merchant cards available for acquisition. The leftmost card is free. When a card is taken, remaining cards slide left and a new card is revealed on the right end from the merchant deck.

The face-up row of 5 point cards (golems) available for claiming. When a card is claimed, remaining cards slide left and a new card is revealed on the right. The leftmost and second positions have bonus tokens.
The space in front of you where cards go after you play them. Cards remain here until you take the Rest action to pick them all back up into your hand.
The merchant cards you hold and can play on your turn. Starts with 2 cards (Create 2 and Upgrade 2) and grows as you acquire more merchant cards. Returned to full size by resting.
Your personal face-down stack of claimed point cards. These are scored at game end for their printed victory point values.
A bonus token worth 3 victory points. Taken whenever a player claims the leftmost point card in the row. A significant scoring bonus that makes the first position highly contested.
A bonus token worth 1 victory point. Taken whenever a player claims the second-leftmost point card in the row. A smaller bonus but still worth considering.
The number printed on each golem card. These values vary and form the primary source of scoring. Higher-cost cards generally award more points.
At game end, each green, turquoise, and magenta crystal remaining on your caravan is worth 1 victory point. Yellow crystals are worth 0.
If two or more players are tied in total victory points, the tied player who took their turn last in the final round wins the game.
The game ends when a player claims their 5th point card (4-5 players) or 6th point card (2-3 players). The current round is completed so all players take equal turns.
The maximum of 10 crystals that can be on your caravan at any time. If you would exceed 10, you must return excess crystals of your choice to the supply.

When a card is taken from the merchant row or point card row, all remaining cards slide to the left to fill the gap, and a new card is revealed from the corresponding deck to fill the rightmost position.
A key mechanic of trade cards. When you play a trade card, you may execute the depicted trade as many times as you want in a single play, provided you can pay the full input cost each time.
